sections.heading: Heuristic Search
sections.body: Informed (or "heuristic") search strategies use extra problem-specific knowledge — a heuristic — to estimate how close a state is to the goal, letting the algorithm make smarter choices about which node to explore next instead of searching blindly.

sections.heading: Evaluation Function
sections.body: An evaluation function assigns a number to each node used to decide expansion order — lower (or higher, depending on convention) values are preferred. Different informed search algorithms differ mainly in how they define this evaluation function.

sections.heading: Cost Function
sections.body: The cost function g(n) represents the known cost to reach a node n from the initial state — the same cumulative path cost tracked by UCS, and one of the two ingredients combined in algorithms like A* (covered later in this unit).

sections.heading: Search Efficiency
sections.body: By using a good heuristic to guide the search toward promising areas of the state space, informed search can find solutions while exploring far fewer nodes than uninformed search — the whole point of adding a heuristic is efficiency, not just correctness.

quiz.question: What distinguishes informed search from uninformed search?
options: Informed search uses a heuristic to estimate closeness to the goal
Informed search never uses a queue

quiz.question: What does g(n) represent in search algorithms?
options: The known cost to reach node n from the initial state
The heuristic estimate to the goal

quiz.question: The main benefit of adding a heuristic to search is:
options: Guaranteed correctness that uninformed search lacks
Improved efficiency — exploring fewer nodes to find a solution
Eliminating the need for a goal test
Making the algorithm use less code
